common_name: Flame Vine (Mexican Flame Vine, Orange Glow Vine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in full sun; flame vine establishes quickly and blooms prolifically in warm weather; it may freeze to the ground in zone 8–9 winters but typically resprouts from the roots in spring.

directions: Root cuttings in moist potting mix and plant after last frost in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; provide a strong fence, arbor, or trellis to support the fast-growing vines, which can reach 10–20 feet; flame vine produces an almost continuous cascade of small, brilliant orange, daisy-like flowers with yellow centers that make it one of the most visually stunning vines for frost-free gardens and one of the top nectar sources for butterflies and hummingbirds in the Gulf South. In frost-free zones it blooms year-round; in zone 9 it blooms spring through fall and may freeze back in cold winters. Nearly maintenance-free once established, tolerating heat, coastal conditions, and drought once rooted. Excellent for screening fences, covering arbors, and cascading over walls.

common_name: Pink Trumpet Tree (Rosy Trumpet Tree, Pink Tabebuia)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring; pink trumpet tree is fast-growing for a tree (2–5 feet per year when young); thrives in coastal south Florida, south Texas, and the Gulf Coast where hard freezes are rare.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in full sun in well-drained soil; pink trumpet tree (Tabebuia rosea) is one of the most spectacular flowering trees of the tropics, producing masses of large, pink to lavender-pink, trumpet-shaped flowers in late winter to early spring when the tree is briefly leafless — creating a breathtaking cloud of color before new leaves emerge. Native to Central America and widely planted across the Caribbean and Gulf Coast, it is exceptionally drought tolerant once established and thrives in the alkaline limestone soils of south Florida. The wood is among the hardest and most durable of any tropical tree. Plant as a specimen tree, street tree, or focal point; the brief but spectacular bloom period makes it one of the most admired flowering trees in zone 10–12 landscapes.
sources: University of Puerto Rico Agricultural Extension Service

common_name: Yellow Trumpet Tree (Yellow Tabebuia, Golden Tree, Ipe Amarelo)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun; formerly classified as Tabebuia chrysantha; the yellow trumpet tree is the national tree of Brazil and one of the most admired ornamental trees for zone 10+ gardens in the United States.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, any-type soil including alkaline limestone soils in frost-free locations; yellow trumpet tree (Handroanthus chrysanthus, formerly Tabebuia chrysantha) is a semi-deciduous tree producing spectacular clusters of brilliant golden-yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers in late winter before the new leaves emerge, creating a breathtaking solid-yellow canopy for one to three weeks — among the most stunning floral displays of any tree in the world. It is the national tree of Brazil (ipê amarelo) and commonly planted as a street and park tree in south Florida and along the Gulf Coast. Exceptionally drought-tolerant, hurricane-resistant, and adaptable to poor soils once established. Plant as a specimen in a lawn or as an avenue tree where its brief but extraordinary bloom can be fully appreciated.

planting.note: Sow in fall for spring germination in a deep, well-drained location; asafoetida is a Central Asian steppe plant requiring deep, dry, alkaline to neutral soil and is not suited to humid Gulf Coast climates — it is most reliably grown in arid Western gardens; in humid climates, grow in raised beds with excellent drainage.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall in deep, perfectly drained, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; asafoetida is a tall, carrot-family perennial (Ferula assa-foetida) native to the dry steppes and mountains of Iran and Afghanistan, grown for the pungent resinous sap (hing) tapped from the roots of mature 4–5 year old plants — used as a cooking spice in South Asian, Middle Eastern, and ancient Roman cuisine that imparts an onion-garlic flavor when cooked in oil despite its famously unpleasant raw odor. The plant requires full sun, perfectly dry, well-drained alkaline soil and is very intolerant of summer humidity and wet conditions, making it challenging to grow outside arid regions. Primarily of interest to specialty herb growers; commercial hing is tapped from plants growing wild in Central Asia.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring or start from seed; prefers dry, alkaline, rocky, poor soil with excellent drainage — perfect drainage is essential as cat thyme rots quickly in wet conditions; a classic Mediterranean rock garden plant.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings or sow seeds in well-drained, poor, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cat thyme is a compact, woody Mediterranean subshrub (despite the name, not a true thyme) with small, oval, intensely aromatic leaves and small pink-purple flowers that attract bees; the plants have a strong, pungent, resinous, slightly camphor-like scent distinct from culinary thyme. As its common name suggests, it attracts cats intensely, similar to catnip but stronger in effect — cats will roll on the plants and sometimes destroy them, so caging or placing them in pots on high shelves is advisable in gardens with cats. Used in traditional herbal medicine and as a garden plant for rocky banks, walls, and Mediterranean-themed plantings. Excellent drainage is non-negotiable.
